vignette: A 55-year-old male with no significant medical history asks about age-appropriate cancer screenings. He is a never-smoker. Which screening is recommended for him?

options.text: Low-dose CT chest for lung cancer

explanation.whyCorrect: CRC screening starts at 45. Lung cancer screening is for smokers ≥20 pack-years.

whyWrong.A: Lung cancer screening is only for heavy smokers.
whyWrong.C: PSA requires shared decision-making.
whyWrong.D: AAA screening is for males 65-75 who ever smoked.
whyWrong.E: CRC screening starts at 45, not 60.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Screenings at 50: CRC (colonoscopy), Lung (if smoker), Breast (mammogram if female).

vignette: A 78-year-old male with advanced dementia is admitted with aspiration pneumonia. His daughter (healthcare proxy) requests 'everything possible be done.' The medical team believes this would be futile. What is the BEST approach?

options.text: Follow the daughter's wishes and intubate if needed
Refuse to intubate as it is medically futile
Consult the ethics committee and arrange a family meeting
Ask the daughter to prove the patient would want this

explanation.whyCorrect: Futility disputes: Ethics consult + Family meeting. Focus on patient's values.

whyWrong.A: Blindly following futile wishes violates ethical duty.
whyWrong.B: Unilaterally refusing damages relationship.
whyWrong.D: Transfer avoids the problem.
whyWrong.E: Confrontational approach escalates conflict.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Futility disputes: Ethics consult + Family meeting. Time-limited trials can help.

vignette: A diagnostic test has sensitivity 95% and specificity 90% for a disease with 2% prevalence. If a patient tests positive, what is the approximate probability they actually have the disease?

whyWrong.A: 95% is sensitivity, not PPV.
whyWrong.B: 90% is specificity, not PPV.
whyWrong.C: 50% would require higher prevalence.
whyWrong.E: 84% is closer to NPV.

explanation.clinicalPearl: PPV depends on PREVALENCE! Even excellent tests have low PPV when disease is rare.

vignette: A 70-year-old male with septic shock receives 30mL/kg IV crystalloid. He remains hypotensive (BP 78/45) despite fluids. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Persistent hypotension after initial fluid resuscitation = Start vasopressors. Norepinephrine is FIRST-LINE (alpha + beta agonist). Target MAP ≥65 mmHg.

whyWrong.A: Additional fluids without vasopressors will not restore perfusion.
whyWrong.C: Dobutamine is for cardiogenic shock with low cardiac output.
whyWrong.D: Hydrocortisone is for refractory shock (despite vasopressors).
whyWrong.E: Transfusion only if Hb <7 and evidence of inadequate oxygen delivery.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Septic shock: Fluids → Norepinephrine (MAP ≥65) → Vasopressin (2nd line) → Hydrocortisone (refractory).

vignette: An ECG shows PR interval 220ms, QRS 140ms with a 'slurred upstroke' (delta wave) in lead II. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: WPW: Short PR (<120ms), wide QRS (>120ms), delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke). Accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypasses AV node causing pre-excitation.

whyWrong.A: First-degree AV block has PROLONGED PR (>200ms), not short PR.
whyWrong.B: LBBB has wide QRS but no delta wave and normal PR.
whyWrong.D: Hyperkalemia causes peaked T waves, loss of P waves.
whyWrong.E: Brugada has ST elevation in V1-V3 with RBBB pattern.

explanation.clinicalPearl: WPW triad: Short PR + Delta wave + Wide QRS. Risk of sudden death if AF develops (rapid conduction via accessory pathway).

vignette: A patient with COPD on home O2 presents with ABG: pH 7.28, PaCO2 75, HCO3 34. What is the acid-base disorder?

options.text: Chronic respiratory acidosis with acute exacerbation

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic respiratory acidosis: Expected HCO3 increase = 4 mEq/L per 10mmHg PaCO2 rise. Baseline PaCO2 ~50, HCO3 ~30. Now: PaCO2 75 (acute rise of 25) with HCO3 34 (appropriate chronic compensation + partial acute). pH 7.28 indicates acute-on-chronic.

whyWrong.A: Acute resp acidosis: pH ↓ 0.08 per 10 PaCO2 rise. HCO3 would be near normal.
whyWrong.C: pH is acidotic but PaCO2 is elevated (respiratory, not metabolic).
whyWrong.D: HCO3 is appropriately elevated for chronic compensation.
whyWrong.E: pH is acidotic, not alkalotic.

explanation.clinicalPearl: COPD: Chronic CO2 retainer with compensated HCO3. Acute rise in CO2 = acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis.

vignette: A 35-year-old male presents with sharp chest pain worse with lying down and improved with leaning forward. He had a cold last week. ECG: Diffuse ST elevation. What is the BEST next step?

options.text: CT chest to rule out PE

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute pericarditis: Positional chest pain + Diffuse ST elevation + Viral prodrome. Treatment: NSAIDs + Colchicine (3 months). Do NOT use steroids first-line.

whyWrong.A: Diffuse (not territorial) ST elevation = pericarditis, not STEMI.
whyWrong.C: PE would cause dyspnea, tachycardia, and normal CXR.
whyWrong.D: Echo may show effusion but diagnosis is clinical + ECG.
whyWrong.E: PPI treats GERD, not pericarditis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pericarditis: Positional CP + Diffuse STE + PR depression. Tx: NSAIDs + Colchicine. NEVER steroids first.

vignette: An 80-year-old male is 2 days post-hip surgery. He is agitated, disoriented, and trying to pull out his IV lines. His son says 'he was fine yesterday.' Symptoms fluctuate throughout the day. What is the BEST diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Delirium: Acute onset (hours-days), fluctuating course, inattention, altered consciousness. Often triggered by surgery, medications, infection, or metabolic disturbance. Reversible if underlying cause treated.

whyWrong.A: Alzheimer: Chronic, progressive, insidious onset over years.
whyWrong.C: Lewy body: Visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuating cognition (chronic).
whyWrong.D: Vascular: Stepwise deterioration, focal neurological signs.
whyWrong.E: FTD: Personality/behavior changes, younger onset.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Delirium = Acute + Fluctuating + Inattention. Dementia = Chronic + Progressive + Memory. Delirium is a medical emergency.

vignette: A patient in septic shock remains hypotensive (MAP 58) despite norepinephrine 0.5 mcg/kg/min. What is the BEST second-line vasopressor?

explanation.whyCorrect: Refractory septic shock on high-dose norepinephrine: Add vasopressin (0.03 units/min fixed dose). Vasopressin is a non-catecholamine vasopressor that potentiates norepinephrine effect and reduces norepinephrine requirements.

whyWrong.A: Dopamine is second-line (more arrhythmias than norepinephrine).
whyWrong.B: Dobutamine is an inotrope (for low cardiac output), not a vasopressor.
whyWrong.D: Phenylephrine is pure alpha-agonist, less effective than vasopressin in sepsis.
whyWrong.E: Milrinone is an inodilator (for cardiogenic shock), would worsen hypotension.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Septic shock: Norepinephrine (1st) → Vasopressin (2nd) → Epinephrine (3rd). Hydrocortisone if refractory to vasopressors.

vignette: A 85-year-old with metastatic pancreatic cancer is in severe pain despite oral morphine. He develops myoclonus and confusion. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Opioid-induced neurotoxicity (OIN): Myoclonus, hyperalgesia, delirium. Caused by morphine metabolite (M3G) accumulation, especially in renal impairment. Rotate to a different opioid (hydromorphone, fentanyl) that lacks neurotoxic metabolites.

whyWrong.A: Increasing dose would worsen neurotoxicity.
whyWrong.C: Naloxone reverses pain control and causes withdrawal crisis.
whyWrong.D: Midazolam treats symptoms but not the underlying cause.
whyWrong.E: Stopping all opioids leaves patient in severe pain (unethical).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Opioid neurotoxicity: Rotate to different opioid. Hydromorphone/fentanyl have less neurotoxic metabolites than morphine.

vignette: A 45-year-old female on OCPs presents with acute pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. HR 105, no hemoptysis. What is the Wells criteria score and next step?

options.text: Wells <2: PE ruled out, no testing

explanation.whyCorrect: Modified Wells criteria: Clinical signs/symptoms of DVT (3), PE is #1 diagnosis or equally likely (3), HR >100 (1.5), immobilization/surgery <4 weeks (1.5), previous DVT/PE (1.5), hemoptysis (1), cancer (1). Her score: HR >100 (1.5) + OCPs (not scored but risk factor) + Clinical suspicion moderate. Wells ≤4: D-dimer → if positive → CTPA. Wells >4: CTPA directly. PERC rule can be used if Wells <2.

whyWrong.A: Wells >4 requires CTPA directly; her score is moderate.
whyWrong.B: Wells 4.5: D-dimer → CTPA if positive. Appropriate algorithm.
whyWrong.C: Wells is moderate (not <2); requires workup.
whyWrong.D: Do not anticoagulate without diagnostic confirmation.
whyWrong.E: V/Q scan is alternative if CTPA contraindicated (renal failure, contrast allergy).

explanation.clinicalPearl: PE workup: Wells ≤4 → D-dimer → CTPA if positive. Wells >4 → CTPA directly. PERC rule if Wells <2. OCPs increase risk.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with gallstone pancreatitis has glucose 250, age 60, WBC 18K, LDH 400, AST 300. What is the Ranson score and prognosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Ranson criteria (at admission): Age >55 (1), WBC >16K (1), Glucose >200 (1), LDH >350 (1), AST >250 (1). His score = 5 (all 5 positive). At 48h: Hct drop >10%, BUN increase >5, Ca <8, PaO2 <60, Base deficit >4, Fluid sequestration >6L. Score 0-2: Mild (mortality <1%), 3-4: Moderate (mortality 15-20%), 5-6: Severe (mortality 40%), ≥7: Very severe (mortality >50%). He needs aggressive fluid resuscitation and ICU monitoring.

whyWrong.A: Score is 5, not 0.
whyWrong.B: Score 5 = Severe pancreatitis. 40% mortality. Needs ICU.
whyWrong.C: Score is 5, not 2.
whyWrong.D: Score is 5, not 1.
whyWrong.E: Ranson criteria are specifically for acute pancreatitis prognosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Ranson ≥3 = Severe pancreatitis. His score = 5 (mortality 40%). Aggressive LR fluids. ICU monitoring.

vignette: A 75-year-old female with AF on warfarin has HTN (uncontrolled, SBP 160), abnormal renal function (Cr 2.0), history of GI bleed, labile INRs, and alcohol use >8 drinks/week. What is her HAS-BLED score?

options.text: Score 5: High risk - Consider DOAC over warfarin, address modifiable risk factors

explanation.whyCorrect: HAS-BLED: HTN (uncontrolled SBP >160) = 1, Abnormal renal/liver (Cr >2.26 or dialysis) = 1, Stroke history = 0, Bleeding history (GI bleed) = 1, Labile INRs = 1, Elderly (age >65) = 1, Drugs (antiplatelet/NSAIDs) = 0, Alcohol (>8 drinks/week) = 1. Total = 5. HAS-BLED ≥3 = HIGH bleeding risk. NOT a contraindication to anticoagulation; address modifiable risk factors (BP control, alcohol, INR stability). Consider DOAC over warfarin.

whyWrong.A: Score is 5, not 2.
whyWrong.B: HAS-BLED 5 = High risk. Address modifiable factors. DOAC preferred.
whyWrong.C: Score is 5, not 1.
whyWrong.D: Score is 5, not 3.
whyWrong.E: Score is 5, not 0.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HAS-BLED ≥3 = High bleeding risk. NOT a reason to withhold anticoagulation. Address modifiable factors. DOAC > Warfarin.

vignette: A 55-year-old diabetic male with leg cellulitis has CRP 200, WBC 18K, Hb 11, Na 130, Cr 2.0, Glucose 300. What is the LRINEC score and interpretation?

options.text: Score 9: High risk for necrotizing fasciitis - Surgical exploration warranted

explanation.whyCorrect: LRINEC (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis): CRP ≥150 (4), WBC 15-25 (1), Hb 11-13.5 (1), Na <135 (2), Cr >1.6 (2), Glucose >180 (1). Total = 9 (CRP 4 + WBC 1 + Hb 1 + Na 2 + Cr 2 + Glucose 1 = 11, but max CRP is 4). Score ≥8: Strongly predictive of necrotizing fasciitis (>75% risk). Surgical exploration is warranted. However, LRINEC has limitations (low sensitivity); clinical judgment (pain out of proportion, crepitus) is paramount.

whyWrong.A: Score ≥8 = High risk, not low.
whyWrong.B: LRINEC ≥8 = High risk for NF. Surgical exploration. Clinical correlation essential.
whyWrong.C: Score 9 is high risk, not intermediate.
whyWrong.D: Score 9 is high risk.
whyWrong.E: CT may delay life-saving surgery; clinical diagnosis sufficient.

explanation.clinicalPearl: LRINEC ≥8 = High risk for necrotizing fasciitis. Clinical diagnosis (pain out of proportion, crepitus) > LRINEC. Do NOT delay surgery.

topic: PECARN Rule for Pediatric Head Trauma
vignette: A 3-year-old boy fell from playground equipment (height 4 feet). He cried immediately, had no loss of consciousness, no vomiting, and is acting normally. No palpable skull fracture. What does PECARN rule recommend?

options.text: No CT head needed - Observation with return precautions

explanation.whyCorrect: PECARN rule for children <2 years: CT if GCS <15, AMS, or palpable skull fracture. For children ≥2 years: CT if GCS <15, AMS, signs of basilar skull fracture, or vomiting. This child is ≥2 years with none of the high-risk criteria = CT NOT recommended. PECARN is highly sensitive (>99%) for clinically important TBI. Observation with return precautions (worsening symptoms, persistent vomiting) is appropriate. Avoid unnecessary radiation in children.

whyWrong.A: No PECARN criteria met; CT not indicated.
whyWrong.B: No high-risk features. Observation with return precautions. Avoid CT radiation.
whyWrong.C: Skull X-ray not recommended for TBI evaluation.
whyWrong.D: No criteria for admission.
whyWrong.E: MRI not indicated; CT is imaging of choice for acute TBI.

explanation.clinicalPearl: PECARN: >99% sensitivity for pediatric TBI. Avoid CT if no high-risk criteria. Observation with return precautions.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with HTN and DM presents with chest pain. ECG shows nonspecific T-wave changes. Initial troponin is normal. Pain is suspicious but not typical. He has 2 cardiac risk factors. What is the HEART score and disposition?

options.text: Score 5: Admit for observation and further testing

explanation.whyCorrect: HEART score: History (suspicious = 1), ECG (nonspecific changes = 1), Age (50 = 1), Risk factors (HTN + DM = 2), Troponin (normal = 0). Total = 5. HEART 4-6: Moderate risk (12-17% MACE at 6 weeks) → Admit for observation, serial troponins, and stress testing. HEART 0-3: Low risk (<2% MACE) → Early discharge. HEART 7-10: High risk (>50% MACE) → Invasive strategy. TIMI and GRACE scores are alternatives.

whyWrong.A: Score is 5, not 2.
whyWrong.B: HEART 5 = Moderate risk. Admit for observation + testing. 12-17% MACE risk.
whyWrong.C: Score is 5, not 1.
whyWrong.D: Score 3 would be low risk; his score is 5 (moderate).
whyWrong.E: Score 7+ would warrant invasive strategy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: HEART Score: 0-3 = Discharge (1.7% MACE). 4-6 = Admit observation (12-17% MACE). 7-10 = Invasive strategy (>50% MACE).

vignette: A 60-year-old male with melena has BUN 45, Hb 10, SBP 105, HR 105. No liver disease or heart failure. What is the Glasgow-Blatchford score and management?

options.text: Score 8: High risk - Admit for urgent endoscopy within 24h

explanation.whyCorrect: Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): BUN 45 (6), Hb 10 (men 12-13 = 1, but 10 <12 = 3 for men), SBP 100-109 (2), HR ≥100 (1). Also: Melena (1), Syncope (0), Liver disease (0), Heart failure (0). His score = 6+3+2+1+1 = 13 (high). GBS >1: Needs intervention. GBS >7: High risk, urgent endoscopy. GBS 0: Low risk, outpatient. Pre-endoscopy PPI is NOT recommended per ACG 2024 (no benefit).

whyWrong.A: GBS 13 is high risk, not low.
whyWrong.B: GBS ≥7 = High risk. Admit for urgent EGD. Octreotide if variceal suspected.
whyWrong.C: GBS 13 is high risk, not low.
whyWrong.D: GBS 13 requires admission and urgent EGD.
whyWrong.E: GBS 13 is high risk, not moderate.

explanation.clinicalPearl: GBS for UGIB: Score 0 = Outpatient. Score ≥1 = Admit. Score ≥7 = Urgent EGD. Pre-endoscopy PPI NOT recommended.

vignette: A 25-year-old male in motor vehicle collision is alert, not intoxicated, has no neurologic deficit, no midline cervical tenderness, and no distracting injury. What does NEXUS recommend?

options.text: C-spine can be cleared clinically without imaging
Keep in C-collar until CT done

explanation.whyCorrect: NEXUS criteria: C-spine can be cleared clinically if ALL 5 are met: 1. No midline cervical tenderness, 2. No focal neurologic deficit, 3. Normal level of alertness, 4. No intoxication, 5. No distracting injury. This patient meets ALL criteria = C-spine clinically cleared. No imaging needed. Canadian C-spine rule is alternative (more sensitive). NEXUS sensitivity 99.6% for clinically significant C-spine injury.

whyWrong.A: No NEXUS criteria met; CT not needed.
whyWrong.B: All 5 NEXUS criteria negative = C-spine cleared without imaging.
whyWrong.C: X-ray not needed if NEXUS negative.
whyWrong.D: MRI not needed if NEXUS negative.
whyWrong.E: C-collar can be removed per NEXUS criteria.

explanation.clinicalPearl: NEXUS: 5 criteria ALL negative = Clear C-spine clinically. No imaging needed. Canadian C-spine rule is alternative.

vignette: A 45-year-old female with Graves disease on methimazole presents with temp 39.5°C, HR 160, confusion, and diarrhea. What is the diagnosis and management?

options.text: Thyroid storm (Burch-Wartofsky ≥45) - PTU + Propranolol + Hydrocortisone + Iodine (after PTU) + ICU

explanation.whyCorrect: Burch-Wartofsky score for thyroid storm: Temp (39.5°C = 20), CNS (confusion = 20), GI (diarrhea = 10), HR (160 = 25), Heart failure (0), Atrial fib (0), Precipitant (missed meds = 10). Total = 85 (≥45 = Thyroid storm). Treatment: PTU (blocks synthesis AND T4→T3), Propranolol (blocks adrenergic symptoms), Hydrocortisone (blocks T4→T3, treats adrenal insufficiency), Iodine AFTER PTU (blocks release). ICU admission. Mortality 10-30%.

whyWrong.A: Score ≥45 = Thyroid storm; not mild hyperthyroidism.
whyWrong.B: Thyroid storm: PTU + Propranolol + Steroids + Iodine. ICU. Burch-Wartofsky ≥45.
whyWrong.C: Rule out infection but primary is thyroid storm.
whyWrong.D: Not anxiety; life-threatening thyroid storm.
whyWrong.E: RAI contraindicated in thyroid storm (worsens acutely).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Burch-Wartofsky ≥45 = Thyroid storm. PTU + Propranolol + Hydrocortisone + Iodine. ICU. Mortality 10-30%.

vignette: A 70-year-old female with CAP has confusion, BUN 28, RR 32, BP 85/60, and age 70. What is CURB-65 score and disposition?

explanation.whyCorrect: CURB-65: Confusion (1), Urea >7 (BUN 28 → urea ~10 = 1), RR ≥30 (1), BP <90/60 (1), Age ≥65 (1). Score = 5. CURB-65 0-1: Outpatient. Score 2: Ward admission. Score ≥3: Severe pneumonia, consider ICU. Score 4-5: ICU admission. PSI/PORT score is alternative for prognosis. She needs ICU for vasopressor support (hypotension).

whyWrong.A: Score is 5, not 1.
whyWrong.B: CURB-65 5 = ICU. All 5 criteria met.
whyWrong.C: Score ≥3 requires ICU consideration; score 5 definitely ICU.
whyWrong.D: Score is 5, not 2.
whyWrong.E: Score is 5, not 4.

explanation.clinicalPearl: CURB-65: 0-1 = Outpatient, 2 = Ward, ≥3 = ICU. Score 4-5: ICU definitely. PSI class IV-V also ICU.

vignette: A 65-year-old male with pneumonia has temp 38.5°C, HR 110, RR 24, WBC 14K. BP is 100/60, GCS 14, Cr 1.8, Bilirubin 2.5, Platelets 110K. Lactate 3.5. What is the SOFA score and sepsis classification?

options.text: SOFA 8 (increase ≥2 from baseline): Sepsis with organ dysfunction

explanation.whyCorrect: Sepsis-3 (2016): Sepsis = Infection + SOFA increase ≥2 from baseline. SOFA components: Resp (PaO2/FiO2 - assume normal = 0), Coag (Plt 110K = 2), Liver (Bili 2.5 = 2), CV (MAP <70 or vasopressors - BP 100/60 MAP ~73 = 0), Neuro (GCS 14 = 1), Renal (Cr 1.8 = 2). Total SOFA = 7 (assuming normal baseline 0-1, increase ≥2). qSOFA is screening tool (RR≥22, SBP≤100, GCS≤13) but not diagnostic. SIRS is outdated for sepsis diagnosis.

whyWrong.A: SOFA increase ≥2 = Sepsis.
whyWrong.B: SOFA ≥7 (increase ≥2 from baseline) = Sepsis with organ dysfunction.
whyWrong.C: qSOFA is screening, not diagnostic.
whyWrong.D: SIRS criteria are outdated (Sepsis-3 replaced them).
whyWrong.E: SOFA increase ≥2 defines sepsis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Sepsis-3: SOFA increase ≥2 = Sepsis. qSOFA for screening. SIRS is obsolete. Lactate >2 indicates hypoperfusion.

vignette: A 30-year-old male with vomiting, abdominal pain, and AMS. Labs: Na 138, Cl 100, HCO3 10, pH 7.25, PaCO2 24. What is the anion gap and MUDPILES differential?

options.text: AG 28: Elevated gap - MUDPILES (Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron/INH, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates)

explanation.whyCorrect: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 138 - (100 + 10) = 28 (normal 8-12). ELEVATED AG metabolic acidosis. Winter formula: Expected PaCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ± 2 = 1.5(10) + 8 = 23 ± 2. Actual PaCO2 = 24 (appropriate respiratory compensation). MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron/INH, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates. Also check osmolar gap (toxic alcohols) and ketones.

whyWrong.A: AG = 28, not 18.
whyWrong.B: AG 28 = Elevated. MUDPILES differential. Winter formula confirms compensation.
whyWrong.C: AG 28 is elevated, not normal (non-gap is AG <12).
whyWrong.D: AG 28 is elevated, not 8.
whyWrong.E: Diarrhea causes non-gap (hyperchloremic) acidosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3). Normal 8-12. MUDPILES for elevated AG. Winter formula: Expected PaCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ± 2.

vignette: A 55-year-old with CHF and left pleural effusion. Pleural fluid: Protein 2.0 (serum 6.0), LDH 80 (serum 200). Is this transudate or exudate?

options.text: Transudate: Pleural/Serum protein 0.33 (<0.5), LDH ratio 0.4 (<0.6) - None of Light's criteria met

explanation.whyCorrect: Light's criteria for exudate (≥1 of 3): 1. Pleural/Serum protein >0.5 (2.0/6.0 = 0.33 - NOT met), 2. Pleural/Serum LDH >0.6 (80/200 = 0.4 - NOT met), 3. Pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit serum LDH (80 < 133 - NOT met). NONE met = Transudate. CHF is most common cause of transudate. Exudate causes: Infection, malignancy, PE, CTD. If transudate suspected but protein ratio borderline, check NT-proBNP or albumin gradient.

whyWrong.A: None of Light's criteria met.
whyWrong.B: All ratios below threshold = Transudate. Consistent with CHF.
whyWrong.C: Clearly transudate (all criteria negative).
whyWrong.D: Empyema is exudate with frank pus.
whyWrong.E: Hemothorax has bloody fluid with Hct >50% blood.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Light's criteria: ≥1 = Exudate. None = Transudate. CHF = Transudate. Infection/malignancy = Exudate.

vignette: A 20-year-old college student has sore throat, fever 38.5°C, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudates, and NO cough. What is the Centor score and management?

options.text: Score 4: Rapid strep test or throat culture; Antibiotics if positive

explanation.whyCorrect: Centor criteria (modified McIsaac adds age): Fever (1), Tender anterior cervical LAD (1), Tonsillar exudates (1), Absence of cough (1). Score = 4. Centor 0-1: No testing (viral). Centor 2-3: Rapid strep test. Centor ≥4: Rapid strep test or empiric antibiotics per some guidelines. Treatment: Penicillin V or Amoxicillin x10 days (prevents rheumatic fever). Azithromycin if penicillin-allergic. Steroids may reduce pain.

whyWrong.A: Score is 4, not 1.
whyWrong.B: Centor 4 = Test or treat. Penicillin x10 days. Prevents rheumatic fever.
whyWrong.C: Score 4 warrants testing; empiric antibiotics acceptable in high prevalence.
whyWrong.D: Score is 4, not 0.
whyWrong.E: Score ≥4 may warrant empiric treatment per some guidelines.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Centor ≥4: Test for GAS. Penicillin x10 days (prevents rheumatic fever). NO antibiotics for Centor 0-1.

vignette: A 72-year-old female with AF has HTN, DM, and prior stroke. What is her CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and anticoagulation recommendation?

options.text: Score 6: Oral anticoagulation strongly recommended (DOAC preferred over warfarin)

explanation.whyCorrect: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: CHF (0), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2 - she is 72, so Age 65-74 = 1), DM (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (0), Sex female (1). Total = 6. CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women: Oral anticoagulation recommended. CHA₂DS₂-VASc 1 in men or 2 in women: Consider anticoagulation. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) preferred over warfarin per ESC 2023. Aspirin is NO LONGER recommended for AF stroke prevention.

whyWrong.A: Score 6 requires anticoagulation, not aspirin.
whyWrong.B: Score 6 = High stroke risk (6.4%/year). DOAC strongly recommended.
whyWrong.C: Score is 6, not 1.
whyWrong.D: Aspirin not recommended for AF stroke prevention regardless of score.
whyWrong.E: DAPT not recommended for AF stroke prevention.

explanation.clinicalPearl: CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women) = Anticoagulation. DOAC > Warfarin. Aspirin NO longer recommended.

vignette: A 20-year-old male has RLQ pain (migrated from periumbilical), anorexia, nausea, tenderness with rebound, temp 38.0°C, WBC 14K with left shift. What is the Alvarado score and management?

options.text: Score 9: Surgery consultation - High probability of appendicitis

explanation.whyCorrect: Alvarado (MANTRELS): Migration of pain (1), Anorexia (1), Nausea/vomiting (1), Tenderness RLQ (2), Rebound tenderness (1), Elevated temp >37.3°C (1), Leukocytosis >10K (1), Left shift (1). Total = 9. Score 1-4: Low risk (observe). Score 5-6: Intermediate (CT or US). Score 7-10: High risk (surgery consultation). In young males with classic presentation, imaging may be optional before surgery per some protocols.

whyWrong.A: Score 9 is high risk, not low.
whyWrong.B: Alvarado 9 = High probability. Surgery consultation. May proceed to OR.
whyWrong.C: Score 9 is too high for observation.
whyWrong.D: Score 9 may not need CT (classic presentation).
whyWrong.E: Score 9 is high risk, not low.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Alvarado: 1-4 = Observe, 5-6 = CT/US, 7-10 = Surgery. MANTRELS: Migration, Anorexia, Nausea, Tenderness, Rebound, Elevated temp, Leukocytosis, Shift.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with cirrhosis has bilirubin 5.0, INR 1.8, Cr 1.5, Na 130. He is not on dialysis. What is the approximate MELD-Na score and transplant priority?

options.text: MELD 22: High priority for liver transplant (MELD >15 qualifies for listing)

explanation.whyCorrect: MELD-Na = 3.78 × ln(Bilirubin) + 11.2 × ln(INR) + 9.57 × ln(Creatinine) + 6.43 + 1.59 × (135 - Na) [if Na <135]. Approximate calculation: ~22 (high priority). MELD >15 qualifies for transplant listing. MELD >25: High priority. MELD >35: Urgent (Status 1B). Hyponatremia increases MELD-Na (adds points for Na <135). MELD-Na replaced original MELD in 2016 for better prediction.

whyWrong.A: MELD is ~22, not 10.
whyWrong.B: MELD 22 qualifies for transplant listing (MELD >15). High priority.
whyWrong.C: MELD 22 is high, not low.
whyWrong.D: MELD is ~22, higher than 15.
whyWrong.E: MELD 22 is high but not urgent (MELD >35).

explanation.clinicalPearl: MELD-Na: Bilirubin, INR, Cr, Na. MELD >15 = List for transplant. MELD >35 = Urgent. Hyponatremia increases score.

vignette: A 60-year-old male post-CABG on heparin has platelets drop from 250K to 75K on day 6. He develops new DVT. No other cause of thrombocytopenia. What is the 4T score and management?

options.text: Score 7: High probability HIT - Stop ALL heparin; Start argatroban or bivalirudin; Check PF4 ELISA

explanation.whyCorrect: 4T score: Thrombocytopenia (Plt drop >50% + nadir ≥20 = 2), Timing (day 5-10 = 2), Thrombosis (new DVT = 2), Other causes (none = 1). Total = 7. Score 0-3: Low probability (HIT unlikely, continue heparin). Score 4-5: Intermediate (check PF4, consider alternative). Score 6-8: High probability (STOP heparin, start DTI, send PF4 ELISA + SRA if available). Do NOT wait for results to stop heparin in high probability.

whyWrong.A: Score 7 = High probability; must stop heparin.
whyWrong.B: 4T 7 = High probability HIT. Stop heparin + Start argatroban + Check PF4.
whyWrong.C: Score is 7, not 3.
whyWrong.D: Score is 7, not 1.
whyWrong.E: Score 7 is high probability; requires immediate action.

explanation.clinicalPearl: 4T ≥6 = High probability HIT. Stop ALL heparin (including flushes, LMWH, heparin-coated catheters). Start DTI. Check PF4.

vignette: A 65-year-old post-op patient is admitted to ICU with temp 39°C, MAP 55, HR 120, RR 30, PaO2 60 on FiO2 0.4, pH 7.2, Na 150, K 5.5, Cr 3.0, Hct 25, WBC 20K, GCS 9. What is the approximate APACHE II score and mortality?

explanation.whyCorrect: APACHE II components: Temperature, MAP, HR, RR, A-a DO2, pH, Na, K, Cr, Hct, WBC, GCS (15-GCS), Age points, Chronic health points. His severe derangements: Temp 39 (+1), MAP 55 (+2), HR 120 (+2), RR 30 (+2), A-a gradient elevated (+3), pH 7.2 (+3), Na 150 (+2), Cr 3 (+4), Hct 25 (+2), WBC 20K (+2), GCS 9 (+6), Age 65 (+3), Post-op emergency surgery (+0). Estimated score ~30. Mortality 50-70%.

whyWrong.A: Multiple severe derangements = High score.
whyWrong.B: APACHE ~30: Multiple organ dysfunction. 50-70% predicted mortality.
whyWrong.C: Severe abnormalities preclude low score.
whyWrong.D: Too many derangements for 15.
whyWrong.E: Score is higher than 20.

explanation.clinicalPearl: APACHE II: Higher score = Higher mortality. Multi-organ dysfunction = APACHE ≥25 (mortality >50%). Used for ICU benchmarking.

vignette: A 70-year-old male with acute left MCA stroke has right hemiplegia, right facial droop, aphasia, and hemineglect. NIHSS is 22. What is the interpretation and management?

options.text: NIHSS 22: Severe stroke - Large vessel occlusion likely; Thrombectomy candidate + tPA if within window

explanation.whyCorrect: NIHSS interpretation: 0 = No stroke, 1-4 = Minor, 5-15 = Moderate, 16-20 = Moderate-severe, 21-42 = Severe. NIHSS ≥6 + LVO (ICA or proximal MCA M1) = Thrombectomy candidate within 24h with perfusion mismatch (DAWN/DEFUSE-3). NIHSS ≥10 strongly predicts LVO. He has NIHSS 22 (severe) with cortical signs (aphasia, neglect) = High probability of LVO. CT/CTA to confirm, then thrombectomy if indicated.

whyWrong.A: NIHSS 22 is severe, not minor.
whyWrong.B: NIHSS 22 = Severe stroke. LVO likely. Thrombectomy + tPA if in window.
whyWrong.C: NIHSS 22 is severe, not moderate.
whyWrong.D: NIHSS 22 is not 0.
whyWrong.E: NIHSS is 22, not 15.

explanation.clinicalPearl: NIHSS: 0 = No stroke, 1-4 = Minor, 5-15 = Moderate, 16-20 = Mod-severe, 21-42 = Severe. NIHSS ≥6 + LVO = Thrombectomy candidate.
